learning_objectives: Describe the security architecture of modern operating systems
Understand kernel vs user space separation and its security implications
readings: NIST SP 800-123 Guide to General Server Security

lecture_notes.introduction: Operating systems are the foundation upon which all software runs. Their security mechanisms form the first line of defense against local and remote attacks.
key_concepts: Kernel mode vs user mode privilege separation
Process isolation and memory protection
Security reference monitor concept
Hardware security features: NX bit, ASLR, SMEP
Trusted Computing Base (TCB)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Demonstrating process isolation by attempting cross-process memory access on Linux and observing kernel enforcement

review.value: Kernel mode has unrestricted hardware access; user mode is restricted. System calls are the controlled interface between them.
Address Space Layout Randomization randomizes memory addresses of key data areas, making exploitation of memory corruption vulnerabilities significantly harder

learning_objectives: Configure Linux file permissions and ownership
Implement SELinux and AppArmor mandatory access controls
Harden a Linux system using CIS benchmarks

lecture_notes.introduction: Linux powers the majority of servers on the internet. Mastering its security features is essential for any cybersecurity professional.
key_concepts: File permissions: rwx, SUID, SGID, sticky bit
User and group management for access control
SELinux: contexts, policies, and enforcement modes
AppArmor: profiles and learning mode
Systemd service hardening directives
lecture_notes.demonstration: Configuring SELinux in enforcing mode, creating custom policies, and observing how they prevent unauthorized access

review.value: Mandatory Access Control implementation that assigns security contexts to all processes and files, enforcing policy even against root
When set on an executable, it runs with the file owner's privileges. Common attack vector if misconfigured on sensitive binaries.

learning_objectives: Understand Windows security architecture including SAM, LSA, and LSASS
Configure Windows Group Policy for security hardening
Implement Windows Defender and AppLocker controls

lecture_notes.introduction: Windows dominates enterprise environments. Understanding its security architecture is critical for defending corporate networks.
key_concepts: Security Account Manager (SAM) and NTLM hashes
Local Security Authority Subsystem (LSASS)
Active Directory security fundamentals
Group Policy Objects (GPOs) for security
Windows Defender Application Control and AppLocker
lecture_notes.demonstration: Configuring AppLocker policies to whitelist approved applications and demonstrating how GPOs enforce password policies
setup_requirements: Windows 10/11 client VM joined to domain

review.value: Handles authentication and stores credentials in memory. Primary target for credential dumping tools like Mimikatz.
Application whitelisting technology that controls which executables, scripts, and DLLs are allowed to run based on path, hash, or publisher

learning_objectives: Design an enterprise patch management strategy
Use vulnerability scanners to identify missing patches
readings: DISA STIG for Ubuntu and Windows

lecture_notes.introduction: Unpatched systems are the easiest targets. A disciplined patch management process is one of the most effective security controls.
key_concepts: Patch management lifecycle: identify, test, deploy, verify
WSUS, SCCM, and Ansible for automated patching
Vulnerability scanning with Nessus and OpenVAS
CVSS scoring and patch prioritization
CIS Benchmarks and DISA STIGs for hardening
lecture_notes.demonstration: Running an OpenVAS scan against an unpatched VM, analyzing results, applying patches, and rescanning to verify remediation
setup_requirements: Intentionally unpatched Windows and Linux VMs

review.value: Common Vulnerability Scoring System rates vulnerabilities 0-10 based on exploitability, impact, and environmental factors to prioritize patching
Security Technical Implementation Guide provides detailed hardening configurations developed by DISA for U.S. Department of Defense systems

learning_objectives: Configure comprehensive logging on Linux and Windows
Analyze audit logs to detect security events

lecture_notes.introduction: Logs are the evidence trail of system activity. Without proper logging and monitoring, breaches go undetected for months.
key_concepts: Linux auditd: rules, log analysis, and aureport
Windows Event Log: Security, System, Application channels
Syslog and rsyslog for centralized logging
Log integrity protection with append-only and remote storage
Key events to monitor: logins, privilege escalation, file access
lecture_notes.demonstration: Configuring auditd rules to detect unauthorized file access, forwarding logs to a central syslog server, and correlating events
setup_requirements: Windows VM with advanced audit policies

review.value: Linux kernel audit framework that monitors system calls. Rules define which events to log, enabling detection of policy violations and intrusions.
Attackers often tamper with logs to cover tracks. Remote logging and append-only file attributes protect log integrity.

learning_objectives: Identify security concerns specific to containers and VMs
Harden Docker containers using security best practices
Understand hypervisor security and VM escape attacks

lecture_notes.introduction: Containers and virtual machines are the building blocks of modern infrastructure. Their security requires understanding isolation boundaries and escape vectors.
key_concepts: Hypervisor types and VM isolation guarantees
VM escape attacks and hypervisor vulnerabilities
Docker security: namespaces, cgroups, capabilities
Container image scanning and supply chain security
Rootless containers and seccomp profiles
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a hardened Docker container with a minimal base image, dropping capabilities, and applying a seccomp profile
setup_requirements: Trivy or Grype for image scanning

review.value: Containers share the host kernel, relying on namespaces and cgroups for isolation. This is weaker than VM isolation where each guest has its own kernel.
Container images may contain vulnerabilities in base OS packages or application dependencies. Scanning with tools like Trivy is essential before deployment.

questions.question: A Linux server is running a web application as root. An attacker exploits an SQL injection vulnerability in the application. Explain the security impact of running the application as root versus running it as a dedicated non-privileged user, and list three specific hardening measures that would limit the blast radius.
questions.answer: Running as root gives the attacker full system access upon exploitation, including reading /etc/shadow, installing rootkits, and pivoting to other systems. Running as a non-privileged user confines the attacker to that user's permissions. Three hardening measures: (1) SELinux/AppArmor policy confining the web application process to only necessary files and network ports. (2) Drop all Linux capabilities except those required (e.g., CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE). (3) Use a read-only filesystem with a dedicated writable tmpfs for temporary data.

rubric.key: Explains root vs non-privileged impact clearly

questions.question: Describe how an attacker would use Mimikatz to extract credentials from a Windows system, and explain two specific defenses that prevent or mitigate this attack.
questions.answer: Mimikatz reads LSASS process memory to extract NTLM hashes, Kerberos tickets, and plaintext passwords stored by WDigest. It requires local administrator or SYSTEM privileges. Defenses: (1) Enable Credential Guard, which uses virtualization-based security to isolate LSASS in a protected container that Mimikatz cannot access. (2) Disable WDigest authentication via registry (UseLogonCredential = 0) to prevent plaintext password caching in LSASS memory.

rubric.key: First valid defense with technical detail
Second valid defense with technical detail

quizzes.topic: Patch Management, Logging, and Container Security

questions.question: An organization's vulnerability scan reveals CVE-2024-1234 with a CVSS base score of 9.8 on a production database server. The vendor patch requires a reboot. Describe a risk-based approach to handling this, including what factors determine the patching timeline.
questions.answer: A CVSS 9.8 is Critical severity, indicating remote code execution with no authentication required. Factors for timeline: (1) Is there active exploitation in the wild (check CISA KEV)? If yes, emergency patch within 24-48 hours. (2) Is the server internet-facing or internal only? Internet-facing increases urgency. (3) What compensating controls exist (WAF rules, network segmentation)? These may buy time. (4) What is the business impact of downtime? Schedule maintenance window. Approach: Deploy compensating controls immediately, test patch in staging within 24 hours, deploy to production in the next available maintenance window (within 72 hours for critical with active exploitation).

rubric.key: Provides realistic timeline with compensating controls

questions.question: A Docker container running as root was compromised. The attacker escaped the container and gained host access. Explain two specific mechanisms that failed and two controls that would have prevented the escape.
questions.answer: Failed mechanisms: (1) Running the container as root with all capabilities, allowing the attacker to use CAP_SYS_ADMIN to mount the host filesystem or exploit kernel vulnerabilities. (2) No seccomp profile, allowing dangerous system calls like mount, ptrace, and unshare. Prevention controls: (1) Run the container as a non-root user (USER directive in Dockerfile) with rootless Docker or Podman, eliminating root-level kernel interaction. (2) Apply a restrictive seccomp profile that blocks unnecessary system calls, and drop all capabilities except those explicitly required.

rubric.key: Two valid prevention controls with detail

major_assignments.title: Operating System Hardening Project
major_assignments.description: Harden both a Linux and Windows server from a default installation to meet CIS Benchmark Level 2 compliance. Document every change with before/after evidence and produce a hardening guide.
objectives: Apply CIS Benchmark hardening standards to Linux and Windows
Verify hardening effectiveness with vulnerability scanning
Document all changes with before/after evidence
Produce a reusable hardening guide for each OS
deliverables: CIS Benchmark compliance report for Linux (before and after)
CIS Benchmark compliance report for Windows (before and after)

criteria: Before and after vulnerability scan comparison
Functional testing confirms services still operate
Explanation of security rationale for each change
Both operating systems hardened to CIS Level 2 compliance
Reusable hardening guide suitable for production deployment
Demonstrates thorough understanding of OS security mechanisms

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of OS security concepts and hardening techniques

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on exercises with Linux and Windows security configuration

major_assignments.description: Comprehensive OS hardening project with CIS Benchmark compliance

midterm_examination.description: Assessment covering OS architecture, access controls, and hardening

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all operating system security topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in a grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab exercises may be completed in pairs. Hardening reports and documentation must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All configuration changes must be performed on designated lab VMs only. Modifying shared infrastructure is prohibited.
course_policies.lab_safety: Students must only modify systems within their assigned lab environment. Unauthorized changes to shared resources will result in disciplinary action.
